aliases: 面筋 (Miàn Jīn) / 素肉 (Sù Ròu)

chinese: 面筋 (Miàn Jīn) / 素肉 (Sù Ròu)

region_of_origin: China (6th century CE Buddhist temple cuisine); term 'seitan' coined in Japan (1960s)
food_subgroup: Wheat protein product — plain steamed or boiled seitan (wheat gluten); meat analogue / plant-based protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for seitan as a distinct food item. FOODON_03310397 (wheat gluten food product) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific seitan / meat analogue node does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat gluten (seitan) — meat analogue; closest FoodEx2 match under wheat protein products / meat analogues category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for seitan. A0EKM (wheat gluten) used as closest defensible parent under EFSA FoodEx2 hierarchy for wheat-derived protein products.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for seitan or wheat gluten food product in current release. Not invented; left null.

serving.description: 100g reference serving, plain steamed or boiled seitan
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical reference serving of plain steamed or boiled seitan is 100g, equivalent to a moderate portion of mock meat at a Chinese vegetarian hawker stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Seitan (wheat gluten, cooked) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy / FoodData Central)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked wheat gluten (seitan). Values represent plain steamed or boiled seitan without added seasoning. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for seitan (wheat gluten, cooked) — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate high-protein wheat gluten product data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI study exists for plain steamed or boiled seitan. GI ~40 (low) is estimated based on: (1) very low carbohydrate content (4g/100g), (2) high protein content (25g/100g) which attenuates glycaemic response, (3) analogy with other low-carbohydrate wheat gluten products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (40 × 4) / 100 = 1.6 (negligible). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium in plain preparation (60mg/100g); risk escalates significantly with typical Singapore hawker braising sauces
hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed or boiled seitan is low in sodium (60mg/100g) and suitable for hypertensive patients, but seitan-based mock meats at Singapore hawker stalls and restaurants are typically braised in soy sauce and dark soy, which can add 400–1,000mg sodium per serving — patients should request plain preparation or limit sauce-heavy mock meat dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~40) and negligible glycemic load (~1.6 per 100g); highest protein density among common plant proteins (25g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seitan has a low glycemic index (~40) and negligible glycemic load (~1.6 per 100g), making it an excellent protein choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes at Chinese vegetarian restaurants — the high protein content (25g/100g) further supports satiety and glycaemic control, provided the dish is not prepared with high-sugar or high-sodium sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), high plant protein; incomplete amino acid profile (lysine-deficient)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain seitan is cardioprotective in profile — zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), and high plant protein (25g/100g) — making it a favourable meat substitute for cardiovascular disease patients, though it should be paired with lysine-rich foods (legumes, quinoa) to compensate for its incomplete amino acid profile, and sauce-heavy restaurant preparations should be avoided due to sodium load.

coeliac_disease.key_factor: Seitan IS pure wheat gluten — absolutely contraindicated in coeliac disease
coeliac_disease.guidance: Seitan must be completely avoided by patients with coeliac disease, non-coeliac gluten sensitivity, or wheat allergy — seitan is pure wheat gluten and even trace amounts can trigger severe autoimmune intestinal damage in coeliac patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (5.2mg/100g), high selenium (30mcg/100g), zero cholesterol, low saturated fat; lysine-deficient incomplete protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Seitan provides high iron (5.2mg/100g) and selenium (30mcg/100g) relevant for antioxidant protection in rheumatoid arthritis, but patients relying heavily on seitan as their primary protein source should complement it with lysine-rich foods such as legumes or quinoa to ensure complete essential amino acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seitan (wheat gluten) is the highest-protein plant food per gram commonly available in Singapore's food landscape, providing 25g of protein per 100g. It is made by washing wheat flour dough with water until the starch is removed, leaving behind the elastic gluten protein network, which is then steamed or boiled. The texture is dense, chewy, and meat-like, making it the backbone of Chinese Buddhist mock meat cuisine. In Singapore, seitan appears at Chinese vegetarian hawker stalls and restaurants as mock duck (kow yok / 扣肉), mock chicken, mock abalone, mock roast pork, and mock BBQ char siu — all using seitan as the primary structural ingredient. The term 'seitan' was coined in the 1960s by Japanese macrobiotic practitioners, but the food itself traces to 6th-century CE China, where Buddhist monks developed wheat gluten products for temple cuisine. Nutritionally, seitan is exceptional for a plant food: 25g protein, only 4g carbohydrate, 2g fat, 118 kcal per 100g. It is rich in iron (5.2mg), calcium (142mg), selenium (30mcg), and phosphorus (170mg). However, seitan is an INCOMPLETE PROTEIN — wheat gluten is severely deficient in lysine, the most limiting essential amino acid. Vegetarians and vegans relying on seitan must pair it with lysine-rich foods such as legumes, tofu, or quinoa. CRITICAL ALLERGEN WARNING: Seitan IS pure wheat gluten. It is absolutely contraindicated in coeliac disease, non-coeliac gluten sensitivity, and wheat allergy. Even small amounts can trigger severe autoimmune intestinal damage in coeliac patients. The glycemic index is low (~40) with a negligible glycemic load (~1.6 per 100g), making it excellent for Type 2 Diabetes management from a glycaemic perspective. Plain seitan is low in sodium (60mg/100g), but restaurant and hawker preparations are typically braised in soy sauce, substantially increasing sodium content.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked wheat gluten (seitan), an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and secondary nutrients are confirmed. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI study exists for plain seitan, but the estimation basis is well-documented and defensible.
notes: 1. ZERO VALUES: sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — plain seitan contains no added or intrinsic sugars. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as seitan is a pure plant protein product. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as seitan is a plant-derived food with no animal-source B12. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked wheat gluten. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1 — detailed fatty acid breakdown for plain cooked seitan is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1 — moisture content for plain cooked seitan not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry consulted. 3. GI ESTIMATION: No direct GI study exists for plain steamed or boiled seitan. GI ~40 estimated as analogous to low-carbohydrate wheat gluten products; glycemic load = (40 × 4) / 100 = 1.6 per 100g. 4. ALLERGEN CRITICAL: Seitan is pure wheat gluten — absolutely contraindicated in coeliac disease, non-coeliac gluten sensitivity, and wheat allergy. 5. INCOMPLETE PROTEIN: Seitan is deficient in lysine; should be paired with legumes or other lysine-rich plant foods for complete amino acid coverage. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodEx2 code A0EKM used as closest defensible parent for wheat gluten under EFSA FoodEx2 hierarchy; exact seitan code does not exist. SNOMED CT left null — no code invented. 7. MULTILINGUAL: This food has Chinese Buddhist and Japanese origins and is widely used in Singapore; Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names provided for Singapore context; Japanese name (麩/ふ) provided for Japanese macrobiotic context. 8. Q8 ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Self-check performed: english (12 entries), chinese (6 entries), malay (3 entries), tamil (2 entries), hindi (2 entries), japanese (2 entries), romanized (5 entries) — all 32 unique strings confirmed present in flat aliases array.
